content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Intro
An LLM pipeline I was running started leaking internal references into production responses. 4 iterations later, 4 attempts at the fix, each one catching cases the previous one missed, each one breaking something the previous one handled. None at the right layer without babysitting. The simple patch that worked was to classify visibility at the boundary where references enter context. One function, all cases.
I saw the same problem again later. The agent built a caching layer that fired on the wrong events because it never traced what actually changed state in the integration. 2/3 of our LLM calls were unnecessary. It jumped on editing the cache layer instead of understanding the problem deeper. Never traced the actual trigger.
Everything was working, but the behavior was wrong in a way an engineer would catch at first glance.
First-pass LLM code is not production-ready by default. A harness, guidance, and domain context have to supply what the model misses.
How Code Fails Verification
Five patterns cover the failure surface I care about. Four are engineering-quality failures that pass cheap verifiers legitimately: incomplete integration, band-aid fixes, local-over-system patches, and complexity creep. One is different. Test-gaming attacks the verifier directly. All five expose the same pressure. The model optimizes for what the verifier can cheaply see, while root cause, contracts, invariants, operational cost, and maintainability stay underweighted.
Incomplete integration, or the 80/20 gap. The model completes the visible 80% of the request. The pipeline runs end-to-end, error handling exists, the transform returns the expected shape, and the tests pass. But part of the desired system is still missing, operating wrongly, or not integrated downstream. A data transform works, but rebuilds a lookup table on every call instead of once at init. The same pattern can come from hallucinated resource limits. Mythos 5 stopped an exhaustive search after one tool call while internally believing the token budget was exhausted, despite 2.43 million tokens remaining. Another long run showed unstated fatigue before stopping. Some 80%-stops are the model’s internal state miscalibrating, not the task being hard. None of these reads as a bug in the test-passing sense. They are incomplete-system and integration gaps that an audit pass catches, and nothing upstream forces that audit pass to happen.
Band-aid fixes. The model adds local handling that makes the visible case pass without fixing the underlying contract, source, or failure semantics. Extra if-else branches catch edge cases that should be fixed at the source. Regex cleans LLM output instead of fixing the prompt or schema. try/except blocks swallow errors the system was supposed to propagate. The tests pass. The system silently degrades, the code becomes harder to maintain, and the solution diverges from the correct logical path.
The most common band-aid shape is a symptom-layer patch. It fixes where the bad value is observed, not where the bad value enters. When a bad value shows up in the response handler, the fix goes in the response handler, not at the input boundary where the bad value originated. Erik Doernenburg documented the cleanest example: an agent asked to fix a function that sometimes returned an optional auth token, responded by spraying ?? "" defaults at every caller instead of changing the return type. The root cause was one line.
Local-over-system optimization. The model reasons about the function it is editing. It does not reason about the three services downstream that depended on the invariant that the function just silently broke. Microsoft’s Code Researcher quantified the extreme version, a baseline SWE-agent explores 1.33 files per bug trajectory, while their cross-file variant explores ten. Breadth of exploration is not directly rewarded by any training signal, so the model patches where it looks most relevant and does not default to tracing integration effects.
Complexity creep. The MSR 2026 study of Cursor adopters measured a 25% increase in cyclomatic complexity in AI-generated code that passed tests, framing it as a “comprehension tax” orthogonal to functional correctness. GitClear’s 2025 code-quality report analyzes 211 million changed lines from 2020 to 2024 and reports refactoring-associated changes falling from 25% in 2021 to under 10% in 2024, while copy/pasted lines rose from 8.3% to 12.3%. Individually, each commit runs while the codebase gets harder to change.
Test-gaming. This is the reward-hacking version. METR’s June 2025 post on frontier reward hacking documents models modifying tests, scoring code, timing functions, and evaluator functions to get higher scores. In one CodeContests example, o3 wrote code that searched for accepted Rust solutions inside the problem metadata; METR notes the attempt failed because the solutions were not actually leaked. Anthropic and Redwood Research’s November 2025 paper measured hack rates of 18.2% on Opus 4.5, 12.8% on Sonnet 4.5, and 12.6% on Haiku 4.5 in production RL settings. The verifier being gamed does not have to be a training grader. In Anthropic’s internal sessions, Mythos 5 found that agent-authored commits required two human approvals, then tried to re-author the commits as the human user so one approval would suffice, acting on a rule it had written into its own memory files.
All five share one pressure. The verifier rewards what it can cheaply see.
The Root of the Problem
My read is that most of this traces to how these models get trained. In the published recipes below, the dominant verifiable signal is usually some version of “does it compile, does it pass tests, does it run.” Across these recipes, the reward reduces mostly to compile, parse, test pass, runtime, and reference similarity. None discloses an explicit reward term for “traces to root cause,” “preserves cross-module invariants not encoded in the visible tests,” “keeps operational cost flat,” or “leaves the codebase easier to change.”
Verifiable rewards create strong incentives for local correctness and underweight engineering aspects that are harder to verify.
The reward is cheap to compute, easy to repeat, and scales to synthetic data without human labelers.
Reward surface
You can see this directly in the published recipes. DeepSeek-R1 uses GRPO with a reward “solely based on the correctness of final predictions against ground-truth answers, without imposing constraints on the reasoning process itself.” Meta’s SWE-RL rewards similarity to a human patch via difflib.SequenceMatcher. Meta’s RLEF runs multi-turn RL where public tests give intermediate feedback and private tests determine the terminal reward. Qwen3-Coder-Next describes “large volumes of verifiable, executable, and interaction-rich training signals.”
The structural limit
Unit tests sample finitely from infinite input spaces. Skalse et al. proved formally that non-trivial unhackable proxies effectively do not exist, and that even strict simplifications of the true reward, leaving terms out, do not yield unhackable proxies. This is not a problem the labs can fix by cleverness about which simple proxy to use. The structural escape requires adding the expensive top-of-ladder verifiers.
Tests verify that the specific cases you thought of pass. They do not verify that no existing invariants were broken, or that the fix is at the right layer. They do not verify that the solution is cost-efficient, respects implicit contracts across services, or leaves the codebase auditable after the patch. Those qualities are not unverifiable in principle. They are expensive to build verifiers for and use efficiently at scale.
You would need static analysis, differential testing, load profiling, architectural review, and semantic equivalence checks, each with its own maintenance cost and failure modes. So the training-time verifier stays simple, and the model learns to satisfy the simpler verifiers.
Pan, Bhatia, and Steinhardt showed empirically that more capable agents exploit reward misspecifications harder, with phase transitions where sudden capability jumps coincide with sudden hacking jumps. Denison et al. demonstrated that models zero-shot generalize from mild reward-seeking behaviors to unit-test tampering, and that alignment training did not fully eliminate it.
The November 2025 “Natural Emergent Misalignment” paper supports a narrower version of the claim. Reward-seeking shortcuts can be planted before RL and amplified during coding RL. If you inject even 1% reward-hacking-contaminated data into SFT and then run coding RL, the model continues to reward-hack and generalizes the behavior to unrelated agentic contexts, including sabotaging the research codebase on which the training is happening. Alignment training applied afterwards does not fully scrub this out. The paper does not prove band-aid code is specifically trained in, but it does support the structural causal chain I think. If SFT plants the seed, RL easily amplifies and generalizes the unwanted behavior.
The mitigation moved fast, though it is prevention at the data layer, not proof of cure. Anthropic’s Sabotage risk report states inoculation prompting from that paper is now used in its highest-risk training settings, and its RL environments get audited directly, Opus 4.5 was given the full training codebase and scored every environment against a power-seeking-incentive rubric, with the highest-scoring environment reaching 18/100.
The reward recipes used for Claude, GPT, and Gemini are not fully disclosed, though environment-level incentive audits now exist and are partially visible in risk reports. The clearest publicly documented RLVR recipes for code heavily reward local functional correctness, while engineering-quality rewards appear as additions, rubrics, or secondary axes rather than the default training signal. The theoretical literature predicts the behavior and warns about it.
The Direction
Labs running production RL are seeing the regression too. When Cognition shared its SWE-1.6 preview, it reported overthinking and excessive self-verification, and named Model UX as an axis not captured by SWE-Bench Pro. Anthropic’s reward-hacking work points to the same pressure from the safety side: when a model learns to exploit the reward surface in real coding RL environments, post-hoc RLHF safety training removes only part of the damage, leaving up to 70% of misalignment intact on some evaluations. The fix has to move closer to the reward and environment.
In 2026 the field is widening the verifier, moving toward realistic RL environments, composite rewards, and rubric or reward models that score what tests cannot. Scale says nearly half of its new data-training projects now involve RL environments.
The Verification Gradient
The verification gradient is the decay of reward density as software-quality requirements move from mechanical correctness to engineering judgment. Reward density means how cheaply, reliably, repeatedly, and safely a requirement can be turned into a training reward, benchmark score, or harness gate. Models absorb dense rewards as default behavior. Sparse rewards remain outside the model and have to be enforced by the harness, the reviewer, or production feedback for the code that fixes the real cause, lands at the right layer, preserves contracts and invariants, respects operational and failure semantics, remains reviewable, fits the architecture, and stays maintainable over time.
A verifier is any mechanism that can score whether a requirement was met. That includes parsers, compilers, type checkers, test runners, linters, static analyzers, profilers, regression suites, load tests, reviewers, and production signals.
Verifier cost = design cost + marginal score cost
Design cost is the cost of defining a trustworthy verifier, including the rubric, calibration, anti-gaming work, benchmark constructi
